title: Global wildlife exports
subtitle: Wildlife trade is quantified in terms of whole organism equivalents (WOE). For example, five skulls represent five WOEs, whereas it's assumed that four ears are sourced from two animals and so represent two WOEs.
caption:
Global wildlife exports

Wildlife trade is quantified in terms of whole organism equivalents (WOE). For example, five skulls represent five WOEs, whereas it's assumed that four ears are sourced from two animals and so represent two WOEs.

This line chart tracks global wildlife exports from 1975 to 2014, measured in millions of whole organism equivalents (WOE). The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 8 million. Two categories dominate the visual space: Reptiles and Invertebrates. The Reptiles line, colored tan, begins near zero in 1975 and rises sharply to reach the chart's absolute peak of 8 million around 1990. Following this peak, the line drops to approximately 5 million in 1992 and fluctuates between 3.5 million and 5.5 million for the remainder of the timeline, ending near 3.8 million in 2014. The Invertebrates line, colored maroon, remains low until the mid-1980s before exhibiting high volatility. It features distinct spikes reaching roughly 5.5 million around 1993 and 6 million around 2006, with a final value near 2.8 million. In contrast, Birds, Fish, Mammals, and Amphibians cluster in the lower section of the chart, consistently staying below the 2 million mark. The Birds line shows a notable peak around 1.8 million in the year 2000 before declining. The Fish line remains mostly flat near the bottom, while Mammals and Amphibians stay close to the zero baseline throughout the forty-year period.
